coredata.dc:title: Functionalized graphene quantum dots for dye-sensitized solar cell: Key challenges, recent developments and future prospects
coredata.dc:creator: Mahalingam, Savisha
Manap, Abreeza
Omar, Azimah
Low, Foo Wah
Afandi, N.F.
Chia, Chin Hua
Rahim, Nasrudin Abd
coredata.dc:description: One of the pioneering materials nowadays is graphene quantum dot (GQD), which possesses outstanding electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties followed by less toxicity and robust photoluminescence. These commendable properties allow GQD to be suitable enough to apply in dye-sensitized solar cells, incapacitating current material limits. Nevertheless, pure GQDs have many challenges in reaching their full potential in DSSC. Chemical modification and functionalization tune the physical and chemical properties, driving GQD towards high-performance DSSC. This is an ideal way of modifying GQD because the chemical adsorption of atoms or molecules prevents the destruction of the carbon network without substitutional impurities. Here, we deliver a short outline of progress in understanding GQD functionalization from theoretical/computational perspectives. This chemically modified GQD is then linked with the performance of DSSC. The review also summarizes the electron transport in GQD-DSSC to minimize back-electron transmission recombination at the photoelectrode/electrolyte edge. The impact, outlook, and future prospects of the GQD-DSSC are included as a conclusion in this review.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the pioneering materials nowadays is graphene quantum dot (GQD), which possesses outstanding electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties followed by less toxicity and robust photoluminescence. These commendable properties allow GQD to be suitable enough to apply in dye-sensitized solar cells, incapacitating current material limits. Nevertheless, pure GQDs have many challenges in reaching their full potential in DSSC. Chemical modification and functionalization tune the physical and chemical properties, driving GQD towards high-performance DSSC. This is an ideal way of modifying GQD because the chemical adsorption of atoms or molecules prevents the destruction of the carbon network without substitutional impurities. Here, we deliver a short outline of progress in understanding GQD functionalization from theoretical/computational perspectives. This chemically modified GQD is then linked with the performance of DSSC. The review also summarizes the electron transport in GQD-DSSC to minimize back-electron transmission recombination at the photoelectrode/electrolyte edge. The impact, outlook, and future prospects of the GQD-DSSC are included as a conclusion in this review.

ce:section.ce:para: The sun's luminous light and heat produce solar energy, which harnesses photovoltaic (PV) technology. Once the sunlight gets diffracted via the atmosphere extending to the Earth's surface, a large number of dispersed lights are produced, and almost 90% of light is scattered during cloudy days [1–4]. Generally, when the intensity of light is slowed down, Si solar cells perform inadequately due to the high reliance on the angle of incidence (AOI) [1]. Thus, it is essential to develop solar cells, which are acceptable to lower the light intensity to widen the global solar cell commercialization. Accordingly, low-light illuminated solar cells are compatible with indoor (portable charging, smart home, and intelligent buildings) and outdoor (outdoor advertising) applications. Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) come up with a low dependency on light intensity and less sensitivity to AOI because the photoelectrode functions as a charge carrier, whereas the sensitizer works a light harvester. As opposed to this, in Si solar cells, silicon plays both roles as a charge transport carrier and light harvester [1,5]. Hence, DSSCs work in indoors and outdoors with low-level lighting [6]. In addition, DSSC's global revenue exceeded USD 16 million in 2014 for emerging portable charging along with solar chargers, solar bags, and wireless keyboards [7]. However, on-grid DSSC modules are still under research and development progress for massive commercialization. This is because several issues, such as instability and toxicity, are limiting DSSC's overall performance and commercialization purposes.

The critical factors for excellent conversion efficiency in DSSCs are due to dye molecules absorbing panchromatic light, electrolytes acquiring good ion transport, fast electron transfer, high surface area of photoelectrode, and electrolytes influencing redox reaction with high efficiency in the counter electrode (CE) [8–11]. These characteristics can be achieved by employing one of the most promising materials in the current market, known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), in different DSSC parts. Recently in 2019, Businesswire reported that the global quantum dot (QD) market is worth USD 3.2 billion and can probably reach USD 10.5 billion by 2025 [12]. Major companies leading in the global GQD market are ZEN Graphene Solutions Ltd, KRI, Inc., Green Science Alliance Co. Ltd., and Dotz Nano Ltd [13]. The main reasons for this great demand for GQD are the strong photoluminescent activity, chemical stability, no toxicity, abundance of the constituent elements on Earth, and high optical absorptivity of GQD. It is also reported as an environmentally safe material.

Ponomarenko first produced GQDs along with the Noble Prize winners, Geim and Novoselov, as the co-workers in 2008 based on the shaping of graphene crystallites to desired sizes via electron-beam lithography with high-resolution [14]. The limitation of requirement for special equipment [15] led numerous researchers to explore for low-cost and straightforward synthesis [16,17] method for GQD with high purity [18,19]. GQDs possess an infinite Bohr diameter, and as a result, random sizes show quantum confinement effect. Thus, GQDs possess a non-zero bandgap and show photoluminescence (PL) upon excitation leading to alignments of HOMO and LUMO energy bands over a wide range of wavelengths spanning from ultraviolet (UV) to the visible to the infrared (IR) [20,21]. These properties are tunable by modifying the size, shape, edge configurations, surface chemistry, and chemical functionalization [22–25].

Thus, these fascinating properties of GQD give high compatibility to be used in DSSCs. Moreover, the size-dependent bandgap is possible to be modified to cover the wide scale of the whole solar radiation spectrum. Large GQDs show absorption edges at longer wavelengths than smaller GQDs [26]. Thus, GQDs can be operated as light-harvesting material. Besides, GQDs hold maximum absorbance of 1.0 × 105 M−1 cm−1 [27], which is composed of imperatively greater magnitude than regular ruthenium dyes and more substantial than traditional fluorophores, suggesting GQDs as ideal candidates for sensitizers in DSSC. Moreover, the incorporation of carbon-based nanomaterials reduces losses of photoelectrons and improves its electrical conductivity in DSSC [28–31]. Most interestingly, GQDs are capable of charge storage, suggesting an inhibition in electron recombination in DSSCs [32]. However, pure GQDs have many challenges in reaching their full potential in DSSC. To expand its full potential, there are several procedures to functionalize GQDs in contemplation of diversifying their properties for specific roles in DSSC.

Among the functionalization methods of GQD include chemical functionalization, where it involves modification in GQDs’ optical, chemical, and electronic properties. It requires amendment of surface chemistry of a material followed by the accumulation of new functions, features, capabilities, or properties [33]. The functionalization types include the growth of composites using inorganic substances or heteroatom dopings and GQDs with monitored shape and size. Chemical functionalization provides a broad scope of benefits to DSSC as following:
1.
The oxygen-containing groups provide high solubility and facilitate further surface functionalization.
2.
Most importantly, it was recently found that due to a lack of further functionalization in graphene and graphene oxide (GO), they have the possibility to become more vulnerable to toxicity [34].
3.
Improves the aggregation limitations providing better interfacing with other materials in DSSC
4.
Increase the affinity of GQD to the surface of metal oxide photoelectrode
5.
Optimizes electronic interaction between metal oxide photoelectrode and GQD
6.
Improves hole transport in electrolyte and device stability
7.
Increases photocatalytic activity in photoelectrode
8.
Increase electrocatalytic activity in CE

Although they demonstrated remarkable optical properties, their performance has not been up to the mark in the case of DSSCs. Apparently, no exhaustive reviews have been done for chemically functionalized GQDs for DSSCs. Hence, despite identifying the cause behind GQDs exhibiting poor performance due to scarcity of recognition in their ability, here we have conducted the link between functionalization and the role of GQD in different DSSC components (photoelectrode, sensitizer, electrolyte, and CE) to highlight the key challenges in obtaining high-performance GQD-DSSC. The review covers a detailed study on the following areas:
1)
Chemical functionalization of GQD
a.
Functional groups (FGs) in GQD
b.
Interaction of FGs with GQD
c.
Properties of GQD
d.
How GQD is cut and formed?
e.
What happens during GQD formation at a molecular level?
2)
Functionalized GQD in DSSC
a.
Characteristics for an efficient DSSC
b.
Role of different FGs of GQD in each DSSC components
c.
Analysis on the reason for low and high efficiency in GQD-DSSC
3)
Link between the chemical functionalization and characteristic properties
a.
How does the functionalization method influence the characteristic properties of GQD?
4)
Electron transport in GQD-DSSC
a.
How does the functionalization of GQD helps in minimizing the back-electron transfer recombination at the interface of photoelectrode/electrolyte?
b.
Detailed analysis of electron movement to efficiently convert photon energy to electrical energy
c.
Suggestion on methods to improve the efficiency of the research works
5)
Impact and future prospects for GQD-DSSC and recommendations for performance enhancement.

GQDs have broad application in emerging PV devices such as perovskite solar cells, organic and inorganic hybrid solar cells, and DSSCs [35]. Moreover, GQDs are widely implemented in graphene-quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs) applications as third material for bulk-heterojunction polymer:fullerene solar cell [36] and Si heterojunction solar cells [37] to improve light conversion efficiency. Incorporation of GQDs in various PV devices are due to having a large specific surface area, tunable band gaps, quantum confinement, environmentally friendly nature, high electron mobility, and an excellent ability to absorb ultraviolet light and convert it into visible light, suitable material as a sensitizer, an electron/hole transport layer, or an active layer additive [35,36]. Therefore, this review would be an excellent knowledge guide for those interested in utilizing GQDs in DSSC and graphene-quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs) applications.

Organic compounds are composed of carbohydrates, fats, proteins, and nucleic acids [38]. Some organic compounds are composed entirely of hydrogen and carbon atoms, and they are called hydrocarbons. When an atom or group of atoms substitutes a hydrogen atom, they are referred to as functional groups (FGs) [39]. The formed compounds are named as derivatives of families of hydrocarbon. Fig. 1 shows the derivatives of families of hydrocarbon containing oxygen, nitrogen, and halogen functionalized groups.

The atoms in the FG are covalently bonded to each other and the rest of the molecule. In brief, there are three main types of FGs, which are oxygenated, nitrogenated, and halogenated groups. The oxygen-containing FGs are carboxylic acids (COOH (OC–O–H)), carbonyl (CO), esters (OC–O), ethers (R-O-R’) and alcohols (O–H) [39,40]. Carboxyl groups are acidic, and the hydrogen atom comes off easily, and the electron pair moves to the oxygen forming carboxylate ion (COO−). Nitrogen-containing groups are nitro compounds (ON +-O-), amides (OC–N–H), and amines (H–N–H, N–H, and N) [41]. Meanwhile, the halogen-containing FGs are acid halides (OC-X) and haloalkanes (R-X), indicating X as F, Cl, Br, or I [42]. The oxygen FGs are polar due to the bonding with oxygen. Besides that, epoxide groups (C–O–C, O–C–C) are non-polar as well as often volatile.

Furthermore, functionalization in chemistry means adding new functions, features, and characteristics that would change surface chemistry. A surface-functionalized material has additional chemical FGs to its surface. Graphene planes are divided into two: (1) parallel surface: basal plane, and (2) perpendicular surface: edge plane [43]. The carbon atoms in the basal plane are firmly attached by highly delocalized π electrons containing covalent bonds throughout the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [44]. Since more reactive dandling bonds are present at the edge locations than the basal plane, they are utilized for covalent attachment with various chemical moieties [45]. The covalent bonding between the edge-site carbon atoms and the moieties provides film-forming capability, imparts solubility, and facilitates post-functionalization [45]. Simultaneously, the basal plane in GQD encountering covalent functionalization leads to the π-π conjugation deformation and changes the physiochemical characteristics [46]. On the other side, the noncovalent functionalization via van der Waals' weak interaction, π-π interaction, and electrostatic force would maintain the graphene plane structure and its electronic properties.

Fig. 2 shows graphene functionalization possibilities at the edge and basal plane of GQD through covalent or non-covalent approaches. Whereby the edge sites of a graphene sheet with dangling bonds are more reactive than its basal plane. Thus, the dangling bonds can be used to covalently bonding with various chemical moieties (Fig. 2 (a)). These chemical moieties can increase the solubility and processability of graphene or provide reactive groups for further chemical functionalization. Moreover, the covalent functionalization of the graphene basal plane (Fig. 2 (b)) could distort π-π conjugation and the associated physicochemical properties. In contrast, the noncovalent functionalization keeps the atomic and electronic structures of graphene, as shown in Fig. 2 (c). Also, the asymmetric functionalization on the two surfaces of a graphene sheet with different moieties (Fig. 2 (d)) could lead to self-assembling of graphene sheets into hierarchically structured materials or provide graphene with specific supramolecular behavior (Fig. 2 (e)) [45,47].

The covalent and noncovalent attachments between the carbon atoms of GQD and the newly introduced material for functionalization occurs via adsorption. Adsorption is the adhesion of the adsorbates (newly introduced molecules for functionalization) from a gas, liquid, or dissolved solids to the surface of GQD [48]. The influence of these adsorbates deliberately changes the electron-carrying properties and conductivity, especially, which lie under the category of electronic properties of GQD trailed by charge carrier concentrations and carrier mobility governing them. Molecular adsorption produces electronic charges, which can be exploited to be used in PV devices intentionally. This has proven to be an essential application. The GQDs/Si heterojunction remains the top-most PV cell occupying power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 16.55% [37,49]. The excellent absorption at UV range with desirable extinction coefficients is larger than the common fluorophores and other QDs. Thus, to carry out the chemical modification on GQD for efficient PV properties, we must be well-versed in the concept of interaction between molecules (adsorbates) and GQD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between functional groups and GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Interaction between the FGs and GQD occurs via adsorption. Adsorption is classified into two primaries, namely, physisorption and chemisorption. Physisorption is typically the noncovalent bonds with weak interaction, where the adsorbates (FGs) have the flexibility to stray around the GQD surface almost spontaneously. The weak bonding (10–100 meV) has longer bond lengths (>2.5 Å) [50]. Chemisorption, on the other hand, is characterized by a strong bond (>0.5 eV) with short bond lengths (1–2.5 Å) [50]. The covalent bonds are chemisorbed due to orbital hybridization from one or more carbon atoms getting converted from sp2 to sp3.

Thus, adsorption energy, E
a is the most crucial aspect of the adsorption process, and it can be defined as in the following equation [51].
(1) Ea = EGQD+adsorbate – (EGQD + Eadsorbate)
Where E
GQD+adsorbate is the total energy of the adsorption system, E
GQD is the energy of the GQD as the absorption takes place (called as adsorbent), and E
adsorbate is the energy whose molecules get adsorbed at the surface. A negative E
a value stipulates for attachment between the adsorbate and the GQD surface.

There are three main adsorption sites for GQD in the basal or edge planes: (1) above a specific carbon atom (Fig. 2 (a)); (2) above the covalent bond of the carbon atoms (Fig. 2 (b)); and (3) inside of the hexagonal carbon ring (Fig. 2 (c)). Physisorption commonly occurs, as shown in Fig. 2 (c), and chemisorption happens as shown in Fig. 2 (a and b). Moreover, doping induced by the adsorbates is one of the essential aspects of physisorption. Doping is attained via the integration of external atoms into the bulk, introducing a hole or additional electron. This type of doping is named substitutional doping, whereas doping due to dopants at the surface getting exchanged with the electrons is known as surface transfer doping.

Substitutional doping is achieved by doping GQD with heteroatoms. It is performed by removing a carbon atom and replacing it with the heteroatoms (noncarbon) via reactive binding sites. The most widely used elements are boron (B), nitrogen (N), aluminum (Al), silicon, phosphorous, and sulfur (S) [50,52–55]. The doping is affected by two different factors, namely the atomic size of the adsorbate and electronegativity [56]. Since B and N have a close atomic size to carb on, they are easily doped in graphene lattice [56,57]. N-doped graphene has no chemical bond with the nitrogen atom and the molecule. On the other hand, B-doped graphene allows chemical bonding with NO2 molecules. Dopants like Al and S are more sp3 hybridized, and the particles chemisorb on the dopant [58]. It is worth highlighting that the very reactive Al atom binds almost with every molecule, which renders the feasibility of Al-doped GQD -based PV cell rather doubtful.

Surface transfer doping includes open-shell adsorbates and closed-shell adsorbates. Open shell adsorbates can only be achieved in two ways. Firstly, due to an interaction with GQD containing carbon atom (chemisorption) covalently to ensure proficient ejection of an electron from a reverberating attachment [59]. Secondly, through weakly bonded adsorbates on GQD. Both ways create a substantial charge transfer, and doping is performed efficiently. NO2, a paramagnetic molecule, induces p-doping in graphene. Alkali metals such as Li, Na, K, and Cs [60] and organic molecules such as tetrathiafulvalene [61] are well-studied n-dopants.

Another way of adsorbates attach to GQD is through the defect sites. Synthesis of GQD introduces lots of defects. More dangling bonds are found at the empty locations (defect sites) and GQD interfaces. Since the carbon atoms are unsaturated, they tend to be very reactive to establish bonds chemically, even involving the evoked molecules. At this time, even the most inert particles such as CO, H2O, and N2 can covalently bond with the defect sites and dope the GQD. Most importantly, the oxygen-containing edge saturation improves the solidity of the edge and provides good adsorption sites for polar molecules [62]. Here, the GQD acts as an electron donor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication techniques of doped and undoped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The produced GQD has different properties in terms of physical, chemical, and optoelectronic. The main reason for this variety is the type of synthesis of GQD: (1) carbon source, (2) solvents and chemicals used, and (3) preparation method. In other words, the properties of the produced GQD depends entirely on the type of GQD-synthesis. Different synthesis provides different kinds of GQD, which is why they are highly heterogenized, unlike the pristine graphene. GQDs have a combination of both hydrophilic and hydrophobic nature. GQDs with individual groups vary in size, edges, defects, and oxidation degree.

Since GQDs are just small fragments of oxidized graphene (<100 nm), they are originated with FGs (mostly oxygen-containing groups) and disordered structures. The oxygenated FGs produced by harsh and oxidative synthesis result in different polarity and different PL emission wavelength. The significant discrepancy between graphene and GQD lies in the optoelectronic properties where graphene oxide has weak PL, while GQD has a strong PL. Defects usually control these optoelectronic apart from the HOMO/LUMO energy levels. The quantum confinement effect in GQD causes size-dependent bandgaps. This effect causes two main factors, which are (1) increment in the energy gap of π and π* with decreasing dot size and (2) energy traps with combined intrinsic and defect emissions [63]. The energy traps suggest that FGs and defects manipulate the PL of GQDs. PL of GQD is a combined effect of the aromatic core (sp2), FGs, and sp3 carbon, including defects (chaotic structures) [63].

Generally, functionalization occurs in GQD synthesis (addition of atoms like oxygen and nitrogen) [64–66] and may occur through consecutive molecular binding when the synthesis is completed [67–71]. The conventional method to synthesize GQD is through a top-down approach, which entails the expansion of popular Hummer's method (oxidative treatment of graphene oxide) [72]). Fig. 3 shows the common synthesis technique of GQD using the modified Hummer's methods [73] and the bottom-up approach of partial pyrolysis of organic matter [18]. According to the modified Hummer's method [72–75], when graphite flakes are added into H2SO4 solution, intercalated graphite compounds (graphite + HSO4 −, SO4 2−, H+) are formed. The oxidizing agent, KMnO4 reacts with H2SO4 to produce newly formed oxygen. This oxygen directly goes into the graphite layer and oxidizes it. The van der Waals forces in graphite moves away from each other and form oxidized graphene.

Based on the equation [75,76] shown in Fig. 3(a), permanganyl ion, MnO3 + is formed and reacts further with manganate (VII) ion, MnO4 − to produce dimanganese heptoxide (Mn2O7). Mn2O7 is a highly reactive dark red oil that detonates when heated above 55 °C or cooperated with organic compounds [77]. Rather than aromatic double bonds in the framework of graphene, unsaturated aliphatic double bonds undergo oxidation via Mn2O7. The MnO4 − attaches with the double bond (π-bond) and forms vicinal diols, C–OH, with further oxidation (Fig. 3(a)). With the help of Mn2O7, it is further oxidized into CO bonds at the basal-plane, leaving a new hole and leads to defects.

Meanwhile, the intercalated compound (sulfonic acid, –RSO3) attached at the graphene edges converts to –COOH groups upon further oxidation [78]. Dimiev and co-workers mentioned that the nascent oxidized graphene contains organosulfate moieties as significant functionalities [79]. Hence, the disruption in sp2 bonding networks is seen upon the oxidation mechanism. However, there are some arguments on the oxidation of graphite flakes. It is claimed that the starting material of graphite flakes consists of various defects specified in its π-structure because it is a mineral that takes place spontaneously, followed by purification to get rid of heteroatomic contamination [80]. This certainty shows that the oxidation does not belong to aromatic systems; however, it reinstates isolated alkenes [76]. Unfortunately, the complication of graphite flakes creates a challenging phenomenon in the explanation of precise oxidation mechanisms.

Next, let's see how the GQDs are cut and separated from a significant size carbon source into small fragments of GQD with less than 100 nm in size (usually less than 50 nm) [81]. The carbonyl pairs (CO) formed at the basal plane tend to create a line on the carbon lattice leading to a linear defect [82], as seen in Fig. 3. This linear defect causes a particular area to be weak and more vulnerable to attack. During the cutting process via chemical routes such as hydrothermal and microwave, it causes the line to break up into smaller fragments of graphene forming functionalized GQD but with less oxygenated FGs than the oxidized graphene. These chemical routes (hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, etc.) cause deoxidation, where the epoxies are removed with very weak carbonyl and carboxyl bonds. The produced GQDs are actually small fragments of reduced graphene from an oxidized one.

However, the bottom-up approach of GQD synthesis is by carbonizing some organic matter by thermal treatment (Fig. 3 (b)). Organic matter such as sugar, polythiophene, and citric acid undergoes pyrolysis to form GQD [82]. Dong et al. studied the carbonization degree of citric acid to prepare GQDs [18]. They reported that GQDs are produced under incomplete carbonization (aqueous orange) because further carbonization may change into a black solid, a graphene oxide. Therefore, different synthesis provides GQD with different properties (size, bandgap, PL, etc.), which means that not all type of GQD is suitable for DSSC. This is because DSSC is composed of four major components with different functionalities [83]. If GQD is applied in DSSC, then the GQD needs to customize its functionalization according to the needs of the DSSC components.

A variety of strategies have been employed to change the properties of GQDs, for example, via doping, noble metal loading, and forming semiconductor composites [84–88]. Mydeen et al. synthesized GQD/ZnO from a hydrothermal method and produced GQD fence layer on ZnO [85]. GQD/ZnO increased the photocatalytic activity, and they observed a red-shift absorption spectrum. Gu et al. co-doped GQD with boron (B) and nitrogen (N) to investigate the electrocatalytic oxidation via solvothermal technique [89]. N and B doping lead to n-type and p-type conductivities, respectively. This synergetic effect enhanced the catalytic activity promoting facile electron transfer.

Moreover, in-situ and ex-situ routes were applied in fabricating polymer/GQD via few simple processes like mechanical mixing, polymerization, and solvothermal routes [90]. They found strong synergism with high photocurrent efficiency, which slows down the electron recombination. Interestingly graphene-silver alloyed QD was prepared via the in-situ hydrothermal method [84]. The bottom-up approach includes citric acid (pyrolysis) and tollens reagent as the primary material. Finally, Gao et al. conducted a simulation study on the compatibility of anthoxanthin dyes in GQD for DSSCs [91]. They concluded GQD-B and GQD-A nanocomposites are the most promising candidate of photosensitizers for DSSC.

The most common characterization instruments performed to identify a particular synthesized GQD is Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), PL spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The characteristic properties of GQD are dependent on the synthesis method paths, such as optical (UV–Vis and PL), chemical (FTIR and XPS), and structural properties (Raman and TEM). The characteristic features of GQD discussed in this section are only used for the DSSC application.

The optical properties of GQD vary from the pristine graphene sheets. Fig. 4 shows different synthesis routes (fusion, intercalation and exfoliation, pyrolysis, and oxidative cutting), which are sourced from different starting materials (coronene, graphite, citric acid, and carbon fiber) produce GQD with various oxidation degrees (zero, low, medium and high). These variations influence the absorption capability of the produced GQD. According to Jeong et al., the absorption spectra show that the produced GQDs lie below 400 nm with strong peaks around 200–250 nm [92]. Interestingly, the absorption peaks shift slightly (minimal changes in wavelengths) towards the right when the oxidation degree increases with decreasing intensity which makes GQD appealing in DSSC. The peak around 230 nm corresponds to the π- π* transition of the sp2 domain of the graphene sheet [93]. Additionally, the pure GQD shows extra peaks at 300 and ~321 nm. The absorption result confirms that GQD efficiently absorbs UV-light, and the peak at 321 nm is attributed to the uniform sp2 clusters of pure GQD. Yan et al. successfully achieved absorption covering the whole solar range by changing molecular precursors [94]. Moreover, the PL peaks shifted towards the right when the oxidation degree increased (Fig. 4(c)) [92].

Moreover, FTIR shows the chemical bonding in the functionalized GQD. FTIR absorption bands of oxygen and nitrogen functionalized GQDs are listed in Table 1 . Pan et al. investigated the chemical bonding in the oxidized graphene and the reduced graphene, which is the GQD synthesized via hydrothermal method [93]. They found that the strong FTIR absorption of the epoxy group at 1052 cm−1 disappeared after the hydrothermal treatment. Besides, the hydrothermal treatment also caused the CO/COOH groups at 1720 cm−1 to be very weak. However, the FTIR and XPS intensity corresponding to the sp2 carbon domain between the oxidized graphene sheet and GQD (reduced hydrothermally), was almost the same. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of GQDs are also listed in Table 1. The N-GQDs showed pyrrole, pyridine, graphite-like N atoms at 398.3 eV, 399.6 eV, and 401.5 eV, respectively [95].

Furthermore, the size and structure of the GQDs can only be viewed through TEM or high resolution-TEM images since GQDs are usually less than 50 nm in size. Pan et al. discovered that the interlayer spacing of (002) plane increased for the oxidized graphene sheet to 0.364 nm and decreased further to 0.343 nm for the hydrothermally reduced graphene (GQD) [93]. The profile of aromatic layers of graphite carbon is 0.3335 nm at (002) plane and 0.21 nm at (100) plane [93]. Table 2 shows that the functionalized GQDs with single heteroatoms have larger lattice spacing, and the interlayer spacing increased with the functionalization of more heteroatoms (0.37 nm) [95]. Surface passivated GQD with PEG also increased the interlayer spacing to 0.278 nm. Besides that, the synthesis of GQD from other sources than graphite increases the interlayer spacing as seen from the carbon fibers and egg white as the carbon source material.

Raman spectroscopy also identifies the structural properties of GQD. The D-band is, and the crystallite size does not follow linearity due to the breakdown of the k-selection rule [96]. This band is related to the dangling bonds present in the end plane of disordered carbon, which retains a beneficial impact on the active defective sites for I3 − reduction [18]. The I
D
/I
G intensity ratio is calculated based on the empirical Tuinstra-Koeing theory [96–98]. A large I
D
/I
G ratio suggests that more structural defects exist in the graphene samples. GQDs produced from carbon fibers have a large I
D
/I
G ratio of around 2.17 because of the high oxidation degree found in the GQDs [92]. On the other hand, the GQD from graphene sources exhibits smaller D-band around 1350-1355 cm−1 than the non-graphene sources. According to Table 2, functionalization with heteroatom doping reduces the Raman shift, and GQDs produced from other sources than graphene such as carbon fibers and egg white exhibit a larger D-band (~1365 cm−1).
Photoelectrode, sensitizer, electrolyte, and counter electrodes are the main components in DSSCs.

The most common photoelectrode material is TiO2 (semiconductor), which is white. This white material absorbs UV-light and reflects all the visible light (red + orange + yellow + green + blue + indigo + violet = white). Since it only absorbs UV-light and considering that there is only a small amount of ultraviolet on the planet Earth, TiO2 is not used for light absorption properties. TiO2 separates the excited state electron away from the hole to avoid excited electron-hole recombination. Generally, the semiconductor acts as a charge carrier. The conduction band (CB) of TiO2 receives an excited electron being infused into it with a typical bandgap around 3.2–3.3 eV [99]. The energetics of CB of TiO2 is influenced by some important factors like local pH, concentration of ions, and the characteristic of electrolyte solvent [99]. However, TiO2 material in DSSC causes random electron transport leading to electron-hole recombination [100]. Modification in photoanodes could demonstrate their effort in enhancing PCE. Photoanodes customized carbon nanostructure curtails the back-electron transfer to electrolyte and improves the electron transport in TiO2 [101,102]. Also, modified photoanodes must be able to change the bandgap of TiO2, which in turn effectively tunes the TiO2 Fermi level to the negative side, producing greater open-circuit voltage (Voc) values. Furthermore, injection and recombination rates rely intensely on the sensitizer/TiO2 heterointerface and the redox and spectroscopic properties of dye [102]. The short-circuit current (Jsc) in DSSC is directly dependent on the charge generation and collection efficiency, ultimately getting influenced by the light-harvesting capacity of the sensitizer and electron injection kinetics, sensitizer rejuvenation and charge transport [82]. For instance, O'Regan et al. attained a PCE of 7.9%, which was good enough when the Jsc was increased up to 12 mA/cm2, high Jsc [102]. Overall, Jsc is dependent on the sensitizer. The following properties covers an efficient photoelectrode:
1.
Greater surface area and rough surface for loading highest possible amount of dye
2.
Possesses high photo corrosion resistance
3.
Optimized bandgap

A suitable sensitizer typically has a D-π-A structure (D = electron donor, which is the dye, π = conjugated linker, which is the FG of the dye, and A = acceptor, which is the metal oxide, usually TiO2). Dye/QD acts as a linker, where the FGs of the dye are the injectors. The most commonly used N719 dye adsorbs through three carboxylic groups [103]. Now the question is, how do TiO2 and dye attaches? Which part of the dye attaches with which atom in TiO2? Once the dye has absorbed photon from the sunlight, the excited electron in LUMO needs to travel from the highest LUMO (highest density/population of excited state electron) to the injector, which is the FG. The part in which the FG will be attached to TiO2 is the anchoring hydrogen in dye. Simultaneously, the proton, H detaches and forms coordination modes of carboxylate fragment (COO−) to metal ions (Ti4+) [103]. The possible adsorption modes (bidentate bridging, monodentate1, and monodentate2) of the –COOH group to the TiO2 surface [103] is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The O–H groups seen in Fig. 5(a): (i) and (ii) (blue arrow) interact with oxygen surface, subtly attached to Ti atom, which is itself affixing to dye. Thus, more –COOH groups provide more channels for the electron to be injected and increases the Jsc.

From the above discussion, a high density of excited-state electron is required to be injected into TiO2. Consequently, we also need a dye/QD that can produce a high number of excited-state electrons when it is exposed to sunlight. As O'Regan and Grätzel [104] came up with the idea of DSSC by imitating the chlorophyll-light absorption phenomenon in plant-photosynthesis, we can compare the absorption nature of chlorophyll for light absorption properties. Solar energy is covered from UV to infra-red wavelengths at the Earth's surface [105]. However, light having longer wavelengths than near infra-red (NIR) light does not have sufficient energy to move electrons to the higher orbitals for chemical reaction initiation. That is why we only consider the wavelengths starting from NIR and below for sensitizers. Chlorophyll is a sensitizer with green color because it absorbs red light and reflects green light. The first excitonic transition of chlorophyll (HOMO 0 to LUMO 0) falls at 680 nm [105] (Fig. 5 (b)). The resultant absorption indicates that chlorophyll only requires deficient energy of light to generate excited electron from HOMO to LUMO, which means it takes only a very low energy to start its operation. In that case, the bandgap of chlorophyll is very small. That is why DSSC is claimed as an excellent low-light solar cell. Moreover, the second excitonic peak falls at 430 nm [106] (HOMO 0 to LUMO 0), specifying that chlorophyll covers the whole visible spectrum.

The most crucial part in PV is the mobilization of the excited state electron. The excited-state electron requires conservation in the excited state, which means the electron should not recombine with the hole in the HOMO. Usually, the excited electrons become unstable during the accomplishment of the higher energy state of LUMO, and they tend to reach a lower energy level. This phenomenon is known as electron relaxation. During electron relaxation, energy is lost as heat. For example, the electron relaxes from LUMO 1 to LUMO 0; then, the electron recombines further back with the hole in the HOMO. This is called radiative recombination, which means it produces light but with a different wavelength. According to the energy conservation principle, the energy emitted by recombination (Eλem) is smaller than the energy absorbed (Eλex) [107]. This radiative recombination occurs because some energy has been released as heat (energy loss). Energy loss can be easily calculated through Stoke's shift by overlapping TiO2+sensitizer absorbance (UV–Vis graph) and emission of QD (PL spectra) [108]. The energy loss due to electron relaxation can be calculated as in the following equation.

where, h = plank's constant (6.626 × 10−34 J) and c = speed of light (3 × 108 m/s). Besides, PL quenching can also be observed through PL spectra. PL quenching is referred to the percentage of QD containing electron getting infused from LUMO into the CB of the metal oxide semiconductor. Two different cases are shown in Fig. 5(d) according to the following equation.
